title: Applying a limewash finish
subtitle: Dilute the putty, brush on the coats, burnish the surface
excerpt: How to apply a traditional limewash to an interior plaster wall: preparing the lime putty dilution, priming the wall surface, applying successive coats by brush, and burnishing to a matte chalk finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). Traditional lime-wash practice described in the National Trust Manual of Housekeeping (open technical guidance). English Heritage guidance on lime-based mortars and plasters (publicly available).

glossaryTerms.definition: Slaked lime (calcium hydroxide) mixed with water to a thick, smooth paste. Used as a binder in traditional plasters and paints. Matures over weeks to months; old lime putty is more workable and less caustic than freshly slaked.

content.text: Limewash is the oldest interior wall finish in Britain. It carbonates as it dries, bonding to the plaster surface by chemical reaction rather than film-forming the way emulsion does. The result is a breathable, matte, slightly uneven finish that varies with the number of coats and the brushing direction. It is the correct treatment for traditional lime plaster, old brick, and natural stone. Modern emulsion traps moisture on these substrates; limewash lets them breathe.

Lime is caustic. Wear protective gloves and goggles when diluting lime putty or handling undiluted limewash. Flush any splashes with running water immediately. Do not apply limewash to gypsum plaster (modern one-coat or multi-finish): it is the right finish for traditional lime plasters and masonry, not for gypsum-based walls.

Available from specialist lime suppliers and some builders' merchants. A 25 kg tub covers approximately 15 to 20 square metres at three coats. The putty must have been stored wet for at least one month; freshly slaked putty is too reactive for brush application.
For diluting the putty to the correct consistency.

Earth pigments (ochre, umber, sienna, red iron oxide) are alkali-stable and suitable for limewash. Modern synthetic colours are not reliably lime-fast. Test any pigment in a small mix; some colours shift significantly on drying.
A traditional limewash brush is a wide, lime-resistant bristle brush. A standard 75 mm decorating brush works adequately.
The working dilution is one part

to two parts water by volume for the first coat (a primer-consistency wash); one part to one and a half parts water for subsequent coats. Mix thoroughly: lime putty settles and the base must be uniform. The consistency should be that of semi-skimmed milk. If pigmented, add the dry pigment to the water before adding the lime putty, not after.

Sponge or mist the wall surface with clean water before applying each coat. A dry lime plaster will suck the water out of the limewash before it can carbonate, leaving a powdery surface that brushes off. The wall should be damp but not wet.

Apply the diluted limewash with the wide brush using broad, crossing strokes. Work the brush in all directions to push the wash into the texture of the plaster. The first coat is thin and the surface may still show through; this is expected. Allow to dry before applying the next coat: minimum 4 hours in a warm room, longer in cool or damp conditions.

Re-dampen the wall between coats. Apply the subsequent coats at the slightly thicker consistency. Three coats is the standard for a sound, fully covered finish. Each coat adds depth and modifies the tone slightly.
What a correct result looks like

Limewash dries to a matte, subtly textured finish that reflects light slightly differently from different angles. Brush marks are part of the result, not a defect. The colour when dry is noticeably lighter than when wet.
The wash dries before it can bond and leaves a powdery surface. Dampen the wall before every coat.
Heavy coats peel away from the surface as they dry. Thin coats, built up, give a stronger, more even result.
Limewash does not bond reliably to gypsum-based surfaces. The correct product for modern gypsum walls is either emulsion or a clay-based paint.
